topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring splash of digital reality against the muted grey of my afternoon. *For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane.* My jaw tightened. I stared at the listing, the faded photo of the porch swing mocking me with its nostalgic sweetness. How dare someone else be contemplating buying the place where I spent every summer, the place that smelled perpetually of dust and sun-baked wood? I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, deliberate breath that tasted like stale coffee. It was absurd. The house was a relic, a monument to simpler times, and now it was just inventory, priced for the modern buyer. I clicked on the details, the numbers flashing past, and felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a hot, prickly sensation that made my palms sweat.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the word barely a whisper, though the air in the cramped apartment felt suddenly thick with my irritation. The online advertisement for Elm Street House had popped up, a digital intrusion into my carefully constructed present. I scrolled through the pictures—the peeling paint, the overgrown garden—and a cold knot formed just beneath my ribs. I traced the outline of the front door on the screen with my finger, a gesture of pure dismissal. I stood up abruptly, pacing the worn rug. Every step was sharp, impatient. This whole thing felt like an insult, a cheap imitation of the genuine memories I held. I jammed my hands into my pockets, feeling the tension radiating up my arms, a stubborn refusal to acknowledge the possibility that this familiar space was now just a commodity.

[story 3]

I didn't even bother reading the description. I just stared at the address, a slow, deliberate roll of my eyes escaping before I could stop it. It was an outrageous thought, this idea that someone else could claim ownership over that patch of overgrown lawn and creaky floorboards. A sharp, almost painful feeling of annoyance sparked behind my eyes. I pushed the laptop away, the screen going dark, and let the silence of the room rush in. I walked to the window, the city noise outside suddenly sounding grating and intrusive. My shoulders hunched slightly, a physical manifestation of the deep-seated displeasure bubbling up. It was ridiculous, this sudden intrusion of commercial interest into something so deeply personal. I turned away, the lingering feeling a hard, unyielding stone in my stomach.
